text: Hello everyone. My name is Weidong Liu. With my colleague Saran, we will we will walk you through the power sequence storage which is scared our training data for the next generation recommender system models. For recommender systems, training data storage is an industry-wide challenge. Models desire exabyte scale data to make better predictions. And scaling the training storage becomes the top priority for the big recommender systems as we seen in these papers. Over the past years, Meta invested heavily into training storage. We have built critical optimizations to keep up with faster scale growth. Our work can be categorized into four categories: columnar compression, row reordering, column reordering, and the model aware placement. We exploit data similarities and the common access patterns to compress the data better and the minimize physical data copies. This optimization work well for the training data with traditional features like dense and sparse features before a new data paradigm appeared. The new data paradigm is user sequence and it changed everything. So, what is a user sequence? It consists of users' raw history of interactions from months to years. It encodes richer user behaviors, content understanding data, and the contextual information. With high data density, user sequences are 100 x or more larger than traditional features. On the modeling side, Meta pivoted to learn from user sequences over the past 3 years for both advertisement and organic content recommendations. We published the market for papers that reported the effectiveness on boosting our 12 AI models. We have seen steadily and the significant model quality improvements as user sequence lens grows to be longer. Back to the data side, user sequence with 100 x larger size than traditional features becomes a single dominant driving factor of training data storage from 2023. Even with all the previous storage optimization mentioned, the growth actually further accelerated. Now, training data is of tens exabytes stored in more than 10 data centers and cost the 10% of meta's power budget. Moreover, in addition to the high cost, this exabyte scale data list many other challenges. First, due to the data center capacity constraints, we are forced to down sample all the time and up front throw valuable training samples away. For the same reason, in many cases, we're only able to keep training samples for 1 month and the much longer term knowledge like seasonality is lost. In the end, we're experimenting new data formats. We need to process and generate huge volume of data, which is both time-consuming and costly. So far, we have discussed that we engineered a strong solution for traditional training data features. However, user sequences change the landscape. The old solution was no longer sufficient. What comes next? We need to rethink about how training data is generated from the ground up. With all that in mind, we will talk about four things. First is the observation data duplication. Next is a solution built on top of that. After that, we will iterate a few critical infra optimizations and the future development. With that, I will hand over to Sarang for the next section. >> Thanks for that for the nice introduction on the AI training stack and setting up the context. Hi, I'm Sarang Mashti. Now, I'm here to share a bit more about the challenges with handling user sequence with our current stack and how we tackle these challenges. But before we go there, I wanted to talk about some basic concepts and a key observation that led to the solution. Let's start with a training row. A training row is a sample that logged for training at the time of inference and captures the complete snapshot of the ML feature used during inference along with the label that sort of indicates how good the prediction was. A training table is a collection of training rows logged over a certain time period. A training preprocessor reads these training rows, reconstructs the training samples, feeds it to the GPU at the time of training. Our previous data model for training relied on fully self-contained rows. That is, each training row stored a complete snapshot of the features that were used at the time of inference. This makes it very easy to reconstruct the training sample at the time of training because all we need to do is read the training row and nothing else. However, there's a downside with this approach, which is data duplication. When we analyze the training data across all our training tables, we find a lot of data duplication. The reason this happens is ML features between training rows that are fairly close to each other in time do not change much and in fact in many cases, they're exactly the same. As a result, there's a lot of data duplication. And this design choice was intentional in the past when our training growth size was small. Since the ML features were highly condensed summaries capturing user behavior and interactions. With user sequences though, the nature of training data completely changed. There was suddenly a desire to store a lot more information about the user interactions and behavior over a much longer time period. Now the training growth was suddenly 100x larger. As a result, our previous data model was no longer scaling and we needed to revisit some of these fundamental assumptions. So what was our solution over here? Our solution to this problem is normalizing training data. And normalization is a very well researched pattern in the relational database world. And it's very relevant here as well. The idea on a very high level is pretty simple. Rather than storing all of the training data in a single table, we split the data into two tables. Specifically, the user sequences, which were duplicated and stored in line in each of the training growth previously, are now moved to a separate table called a user sequence table where each user sequence is addressed by a unique user sequence ID. The original training table containing training growth now no longer store these features in line within the row, but store a lightweight pointer to the rows in the sequence table. With this, we can reduce data duplication significantly, but however, there is a trade-off over here. And the trade-off is there is a more complex training sample reconstruction. For reconstructing training samples now, the training preprocessor needs to do join reading a training row from the training table and the user sequence from the user sequence table to reconstruct these training samples on the fly at the time of training. The entire solution becomes cost effective only if we are able to optimize these joins and we'll look into how we optimize these joints in a bit. Now, with that background, let's take a look at the solution for handling user sequences. Sequence storage. Sequence storage is the system that we built for handling user sequences. And this is depicted pictorially as shown on the slide. Like I mentioned before, training data is now split into two tables. The training table containing the training rows with lightweight references to keys in the user sequence table continues to remain on warehouse backed by warm storage HDD. The user sequence table is moved to an efficient indexable storage since the access pattern we expect on this table to be point lookups. We decided to leverage ZippyDB, which is a widely used distributed key-value store within Meta for this indexable storage. On the train preprocessor side, we now needed a special connector that does a join between the training table and the user sequence table to reconstruct these training samples at the time of training. The first version of sequence storage that we built and integrated into our training pipeline turned out to be a lot more inefficient than we expected it to be. In fact, the overall system turned out to be as costly or if not more as the original approach of storing all of the data in a single table. In hindsight, this was expected because ZippyDB was never used for training workloads, and most of the optimizations over there were for mostly OLTP-ish like workloads. On the training preprocessor side as well, we had now a more complex operation to reconstruct training samples. Hence, we had to land a lot of optimization on both the training preprocessor side and the storage server side to make sequence storage cost-effective and practical. In this talk, we'll be mostly focusing on the optimizations that we landed on the storage server side. Now, with that background, let's take a look at some of the major inefficiencies that we identified on the storage server side for AI training workload and how we address these inefficiencies. On the storage server side, we were looking at a major read throughput optimization challenge with a very aggressive target. Overall, we wanted to reduce the cost per gigabyte per second of training throughput that we could get from the storage server. And we identified three major sources of inefficiencies that we had to optimize in order to hit our throughput goals. On a very high level, these inefficiencies were high read amplification. We noticed that the storage servers had higher read amplification for AI training workloads. As a result, this limited the effective throughput that we could get from the storage servers and we had to land a lot of optimizations to reduce this amplification. The second one was high read latency due to the index join. We had to optimize the index join to reduce the latency and remove any stalls in our data pipeline and ensure the GPUs were well utilized. And finally, there was a problem of data over fetching. We had to land a few features to reduce over fetching of data and improve the client side cost. Now, I will hand off to Verena to talk about some of these optimizations in a bit more detail. >> Thanks, Sura. When we populate the initial storage, we used the row by row insertions. So, we observed that this triggered the frequent background compactions in RocksDB. And the compaction ate much of the DB DB server resources like a flash bandwidth and the CPU. Such resources usage compete with the resources that they used for serving read. In addition, such insertions resulted with a log structured merge tree with high depth as shown on the right hand. Each read for user sequence needs to touch multiple SSTable files from the top level to the bottom level. So, this multi-level log structured tree traversal end up with tags read amplifications here in average on the storage media. Both factors combined severely limit the storage server read throughput because we need to budget a substantial amount of resources on serving writes as well as we much amplify the flash IO usage per read. For this challenge, we tackle it by introducing a new offloaded SSTable compaction and optimization pass. In the VDDV, we introduced a new API that allows direct load pre-generated SSTables from external completely bypassing the expensive memtable and the write-ahead logs in the server from the row-by-row insertion pass. Moreover, we make our query engines like Spark and Presto to recognize SSTable files. On the top, we are able to build data pipelines to periodically compact, optimize, and index data outside of the VDDV servers. Once everything is done, we have our final SSTable files. We are able to load the newly generated files into VDDV servers and the replace the older ones. This resulted with a single look structured the merge tree optimized for read. Every single read now is only served from a single SST table file. We pretty much achieved one IO per read. For the second challenge, high read latency, we looked closely to be that the scans were executed in serial. This was because RocksDB doesn't support native multiple scans and ZippyDB had to do the scans serially. With that implementation, when querying for batch of user sequences, the total latency was the sum of all individual scans. This high latency led to training GPU QPS regression risks since we need to wait longer for fetching user sequences. Our solution was to implement native multi-scan implementation in RocksDB. When a batch of reads are received via a new multi-scan API, RocksDB first taking sight into adjacent scan ranges to identify IO coalescing opportunities. Meaning like if two IOs are hitting the adjacent disk blocks and whether we can do two instead of doing two scans, we do one scan instead. After identifying the coalesced ranges, we issue those IOs in parallel against these ranges. This reduced the storage media IO IOPS and improved the query latency. Now, the total latency is only decided by the single slowest scan from multiple scans. And therefore, this reduced the latency, increased the training GPU QPS. Last challenge was data over fetching. In traditional RocksDB and GBDV, keys and values are treated as opaque blobs and not schematized. On the other side, user sequences is basically a time series of events. Think about a list of structs. Each event consists of multiple attributes. Most of our models only needs to access interested events within certain time ranges and a subset of event attributes instead of all the attributes. If this knowledge was not leveraged, we need to fetch the entire sequence back to GPU and then filter the out uninterested data on the GPU side. This over fetching effect increased the network throughput because we basically passed more data over and they also added the significant CPU and the latency tax on the GPU side for the filtering. To optimize, we made the GBDV query engine to recognize schematized the data so that it can be called serialized the user sequence key values on demand. On the top, we implement feature projection to filter out uninterested event attributes after decoding the key values. In addition, we implement the length truncation to keep only the events within the specific time ranges that matters to specific training samples. Both optimizations in combination reduced the data to be fetched from GBDV to about 1/3 compared to the original situation. Due to the time constraints, we have been brief on the technical stuff. We'll post an engineering blog in more details. Now, I will pass to Saran again to conclude this talk. >> Thanks, Wei-Ning, for talking about some of the optimizations that we landed on the storage server side. With all of these optimizations landed, where did we end up with? We ended up with a storage server that was roughly 5x more efficient than when we started. Now, each storage server was able to support more than a gigabyte per second read throughput up from 200 megabytes per second when we started. And this significantly reduced the cost of the overall setup. We were also able to scale the user sequence length from 2K events to 80K events, which is almost a 40x improvement without significant increase in cost. Sequence storage is currently in production serving 30 plus models across various product groups. With that, let's take a look at some of the key takeaways for the talk. To summarize, we have some interesting takeaways. First one, training data scale is an important problem and will continue to be an important problem at Meta. We are already at the limit in terms of how much data we can afford to store, and workloads such as user sequences are trying to push this limit by almost 5 to 10x. Hence, there is an immediate need to deal with this growth in a cost-effective way. Second, normalization is the right direction forward. Previously, when ML were highly condensed summaries capturing user behavior and interaction, we could afford data duplication in order to optimize IO. However, with user sequences, where there is a desire to store a lot more data, data duplication becomes cost prohibitive. So, normalization is the only path forward. Finally, handling these emerging AI/ML workload often require full-stack innovation. For dealing with user sequences, we had to rework pretty much entire air training stack and land optimization both on the client and the storage server side in order to make this solution cost-effective and practical. Now, let's take a look at what's in store for sequence storage in the future. We have two major areas of investment. The first one is scaling sequence length. Today, we only store about 80K events on sequence storage. But our North Star is to increase this by almost 10x to capture lifetime user history. This scale will bring along with it more challenges in cost, reliability, and latency. We need to reach out to techniques such as smarter encodings, tearing, etc. to make this work. This is still an ongoing exploratory work where we are trying to understand the limits of the current system and how much more we can scale. The second area of investment is to see if we can generalize sequence storage so that is it is more broadly applicable beyond just user sequences. There's an interest to leverage whatever we have built for sequence storage to handle other kinds of features such as item features, content features, and embeddings. Our North Star is a canonical storage for all of the normalized training data and the meta. Thank you.
